questions.prompt: Two equations sharing the exact same solution set, often produced by adding the same quantity to both sides or by multiplying both sides by a nonzero constant.
questions.explanation: Equivalent equations have identical solution sets. The Addition Property of Equality (if a=b then a+c=b+c) and the Multiplication Property of Equality (if a=b and c is not zero then ac=bc) generate equivalents. Worked example: 2x+5=13 and 2x=8 and x=4 are all equivalent. NY A-REI.1 requires students to justify each step. Regents items often display a 4-line solution and ask which property justifies a given step — confirm both sides changed by the same operation.

questions.prompt: The complete collection of values that, when substituted for the variable, make the equation a true statement.
questions.explanation: The solution set of 3x-7=8 is {5} because 3(5)-7=8. For inequalities like 2x<10 the solution set is {x | x<5}, an infinite set graphed on a number line. NY A-REI.3 requires solving linear equations and inequalities in one variable. Regents Part I items typically ask students to identify which value from a choice list belongs to the solution set; substitute and verify before bubbling.

questions.prompt: An equation written with several letters, like A=lw or C=2*pi*r, that students rearrange to isolate one specified variable.
questions.explanation: Literal equations use the same Properties of Equality but treat other letters as constants. Worked example: solve d=rt for t — divide both sides by r to get t=d/r. NY A-CED.4 (Create Equations) requires rearranging formulas to highlight a quantity of interest. Regents Part II often asks for the formula solved for a different variable; watch for sign flips when dividing by a negative coefficient like in F=(9/5)C+32 solved for C.

questions.prompt: An equation that is true for every real value of the variable, producing infinitely many solutions, such as 2(x+3)=2x+6.
questions.explanation: Identities reduce to a true statement like 0=0 or 6=6 after simplification. Distribute and combine like terms: 2(x+3)=2x+6 becomes 2x+6=2x+6 which is true for all real x. NY A-REI.1 expects students to recognize identities. On the Regents, an item might show 4(x-1)+5=4x+1 and ask for the number of solutions; the answer is 'infinitely many' or 'all real numbers' — not zero and not one.

questions.prompt: An equation with no solution because the simplification produces a false numerical statement like 5=8.
questions.explanation: Contradictions arise when variable terms cancel but the constants disagree. Example: 3x+4=3x+9 simplifies to 4=9, which is false, so the solution set is the empty set. NY A-REI.1 requires students to distinguish identities from contradictions. Regents items often test this by asking 'How many solutions does this equation have?' with the trap answer being one extracted by ignoring the contradiction. Always finish simplifying before deciding.

questions.prompt: The opposite arithmetic action used to undo a step when isolating a variable — addition reverses subtraction and multiplication reverses division.
questions.explanation: Inverse operations isolate the variable using Properties of Equality. Worked example: solve 2x+5=13 by subtracting 5 (inverse of +5) to get 2x=8, then dividing by 2 (inverse of *2) to get x=4. NY A-REI.3 requires this fluency. Regents items often present a 3 or 4-step linear equation; students who reverse PEMDAS-style (undo addition, then multiplication) score full credit. Always perform the same inverse on both sides.

questions.prompt: The rule a(b+c)=ab+ac used to multiply a factor across each term inside parentheses before solving.
questions.explanation: The Distributive Property eliminates parentheses. Worked example: 3(x+4)=21 distributes to 3x+12=21, then x=3. NY A-SSE.1 and A-REI.3 both require this. Common trap: forgetting to distribute a negative — -2(x-5) equals -2x+10, not -2x-10. Regents items often hide distribution inside a 4-step equation. The 2x+5 distractor for 3(x+4) tests whether students multiplied only the first term.

questions.prompt: The simplification step where coefficients of terms with identical variable parts are added or subtracted: 5x+3x equals 8x.
questions.explanation: Like terms share the same variable raised to the same power. Worked example: 4x+7-2x+1 combines to 2x+8. Constants combine with constants and x-terms with x-terms. NY A-SSE.3 requires producing equivalent expressions. Regents Part I items often place like terms on opposite sides — like 7x-3=4x+9 — requiring students to move variables to one side (3x=12, so x=4). Color-code or underline matching terms on the test booklet.

questions.prompt: The technique of multiplying both sides of an equation by the least common denominator to eliminate fractional coefficients.
questions.explanation: Multiply every term by the LCD to remove fractions. Worked example: x/3+x/4=7 has LCD 12, so multiplying gives 4x+3x=84, then 7x=84, so x=12. NY A-REI.3 includes this skill. Regents items often present rational coefficients to test LCD fluency. Common error: multiplying only one term — every term, including the constant on the other side, must be multiplied by the LCD or the equation is no longer equivalent.

questions.prompt: The verification step where the candidate value is substituted back into the original equation to confirm both sides are equal.
questions.explanation: Always substitute to verify. Worked example: for 2x+5=13 check x=4 by computing 2(4)+5=13 (true). NY A-REI.1 makes checking part of justification. Regents Part II (2-point) items award one point for the correct value and one for showing the check or full algebraic work. Forgetting to check loses points on rational and radical equations where extraneous answers (values that satisfy the derived form but not the original) are introduced.

questions.prompt: One of the relations <, >, less-than-or-equal, or greater-than-or-equal, that compares two expressions without claiming exact equality.
questions.explanation: Inequality symbols compare quantities. Strict symbols < and > use open circles or dashed boundaries; less-than-or-equal and greater-than-or-equal use closed circles or solid boundaries. NY A-REI.3 covers solving linear inequalities. Worked example: x>3 is graphed with an open circle at 3 and an arrow right. Regents items test reading and writing — the phrase 'at most 50' translates to less-than-or-equal 50, and 'more than 50' translates to >50.

questions.prompt: A statement joining two simple inequalities with the words AND or OR, such as -2 less-than-or-equal x<5.
questions.explanation: Compound inequalities with AND produce intersections (overlap), and with OR produce unions. Worked example: -2 less-than-or-equal x<5 means x is between -2 and 5, including -2 but excluding 5. NY A-REI.3 includes compound forms. Regents items often display number-line graphs; the student must write the matching compound inequality. The OR form like x<-1 OR x>4 produces two separate rays — never collapse it into a single conjoined statement.

questions.prompt: A bracket-and-parenthesis shorthand for solution sets where square brackets include endpoints and parentheses exclude them.
questions.explanation: Interval notation compresses inequalities. Worked example: -2 less-than-or-equal x<5 becomes [-2, 5). Use ( and ) for strict and infinity, and [ and ] for closed endpoints. NY A-REI references this in modeling. While the Regents typically uses inequality notation, classroom and SAT-aligned items use intervals. Common error: writing [3, infinity] — infinity always takes a parenthesis because infinity is not a number that can be reached.

questions.prompt: The endpoint value where an inequality switches from true to false, marked with an open or closed circle on a number line.
questions.explanation: The boundary is the solution to the related equation. Worked example: for 2x+1>7 the boundary is x=3 (from 2x+1=7), drawn as an open circle since the inequality is strict. NY A-REI.10-12 connects boundaries to graphs. Regents items present a graph with a marked endpoint and ask students to identify the inequality. A closed circle indicates the endpoint is included (less-than-or-equal or greater-than-or-equal); open indicates strict < or >.

questions.prompt: The requirement that when an inequality is multiplied or divided by a negative number, the inequality symbol must be flipped.
questions.explanation: Negatives reverse inequality direction. Worked example: -3x<12 divides by -3 and flips to x>-4 (not x<-4). NY A-REI.3 requires this rule. Regents Part I items deliberately include a negative coefficient — the common wrong-answer set keeps the original symbol direction. Adding or subtracting a negative does NOT flip; only multiplying or dividing by a negative does. Verify with a test point like x=0.

questions.prompt: A real-world restriction — like a budget cap or a non-negative quantity — that limits which solutions are meaningful.
questions.explanation: Constraints filter algebraic solutions for context. Worked example: if x represents the number of tickets sold, the constraint x is greater-than-or-equal 0 and x is an integer eliminates negative or fractional answers. NY A-CED.3 requires representing constraints with inequalities. Regents modeling items award credit only for solutions that respect the constraint — an algebraic answer of -2.5 tickets must be rejected with reasoning, not just listed.

questions.prompt: A ratio expressed per one unit of the second quantity, such as 60 miles per 1 hour, often appearing as the slope in a linear model.
questions.explanation: Unit rates connect tables, graphs, and equations. Worked example: $7.50 for 3 pounds is a unit rate of $2.50/pound, so cost c=2.50p models the relationship. NY F-IF.6 and 8.EE.5 use unit rates as slope. Regents items present tables where students must compute (change in y)/(change in x). Common error: writing pounds/dollar instead of dollar/pound — always check which quantity is per one of the other.

questions.prompt: The output of a linear model when the input is zero, equal to the y-intercept and to the constant term b in y=mx+b.
questions.explanation: Starting value is the y-intercept of a linear model. Worked example: in C=15+0.10m (cost = base fee + per-mile rate), the starting value is $15 — the fee charged before any miles are driven. NY F-LE.2 and F-IF.7 cover this interpretation. Regents items ask students to identify and interpret b in context — the answer must include units (dollars, students, gallons) and reference what the value means at time zero.

questions.prompt: A linear equation of the form C=mx+b where m is a per-item rate and b is a fixed fee that together produce total cost.
questions.explanation: Total cost combines fixed and variable components. Worked example: a taxi charges $3 base plus $2/mile, so C=2m+3. After 5 miles, C=$13. NY A-CED.2 requires writing such equations from situations. Regents Part II problems often ask students to (a) write the equation, (b) compute cost for a specific input, and (c) solve C=value for the input mileage — verify each piece independently to maximize partial credit.

questions.prompt: The input value where two linear cost or revenue models produce the same output, found by setting the equations equal.
questions.explanation: Break-even is where two lines intersect, indicating equal cost or zero profit. Worked example: company A charges 5+2x, company B charges 8+1.50x; setting 5+2x=8+1.50x yields 0.50x=3, so x=6 — at 6 units the prices match. NY A-REI.6 (systems) supports this. Regents items often phrase it as 'after how many months will both plans cost the same?' — the answer comes from solving the system and must include units (months, miles, hours).

questions.prompt: Step-by-step written work showing each property (Addition, Multiplication, Distributive) used to transform an equation, required for full credit on Part II.
questions.explanation: Justification means naming the property used at each step. Worked example: solving 3x+2=11 — Step 1: Subtract 2 (Subtraction Property of Equality), Step 2: Divide by 3 (Division Property of Equality), giving x=3. NY A-REI.1 explicitly requires justification. Regents 2-point items award one point for correct answer and one for valid algebraic work; an answer without supporting steps maxes at one point even if numerically correct.

questions.prompt: The process of replacing the variable with the proposed solution and evaluating both sides to confirm equality.
questions.explanation: Substitution verifies a candidate solution. Worked example: for 4x-7=9, check x=4 by computing 4(4)-7=9 (true). NY A-REI.1 makes checking a verification standard. Regents constructed-response items frequently award an explicit point for the check. Students should perform substitution on the ORIGINAL equation, not a simplified version — errors made during simplification will not be caught if the check uses the wrong form.

questions.prompt: A limit on input values that excludes those making the equation undefined or contextually impossible, like negative time or division by zero.
questions.explanation: Domain restrictions arise from context and from algebra. Worked example: in t=d/r, r cannot equal 0 (division undefined). For a population model P=20+5t, t is greater-than-or-equal 0 since time is non-negative. NY F-IF.5 covers interpreting domain in context. Regents items expect students to state restrictions explicitly: 'x is greater-than-or-equal 0 because x represents the number of students sold tickets, which cannot be negative.'

questions.prompt: An algebraic answer that satisfies real-world constraints — non-negative, integer when counting, within the stated range — selected from a possibly larger algebraic solution set.
questions.explanation: Reasonable solutions match context. Worked example: if x represents the number of seats and algebra yields x=-3 or x=7, only x=7 is reasonable because seat counts must be a positive integer. NY A-CED.3 requires interpretation. Regents items deliberately produce both reasonable and unreasonable algebraic solutions, then ask which is appropriate for the context. Discarding the impossible answer with a written reason earns the explanation point.

questions.prompt: A value produced by valid algebraic steps that fails to satisfy the original equation, often arising when squaring both sides or clearing rational denominators.
questions.explanation: Extraneous solutions appear from non-reversible operations. Worked example: solving sqrt(x)=x-6 by squaring gives x=x^2-12x+36, leading to x=4 or x=9 — but x=4 does NOT satisfy the original (sqrt(4)=2 not equal -2), so x=4 is extraneous. NY A-REI.2 requires checking. The Regents penalizes uncheckedanswers; always substitute back into the ORIGINAL form to filter extraneous values.

questions.prompt: The constant rate of change of a linear function, computed as rise over run or (y2-y1)/(x2-x1) between any two points on the line.
questions.explanation: Slope measures steepness and direction. Worked example: the line through (2,3) and (6,11) has slope (11-3)/(6-2)=8/4=2. NY 8.F.4 and F-IF.6 require this. Positive slope rises left-to-right; negative slope falls; zero slope is horizontal; undefined slope is vertical. Regents items ask students to compute slope from two points, a table, or a graph — and to interpret slope in context (dollars per hour, miles per gallon).

questions.prompt: The point (0, b) where a graph crosses the y-axis, found by setting x=0 in the equation.
questions.explanation: The y-intercept is the output when x=0. Worked example: in y=3x+7, the y-intercept is (0, 7) — confirm by substituting x=0 to get y=7. NY F-IF.4 and F-LE.2 emphasize interpretation. In a context like cost C=2x+15, the y-intercept $15 represents the fixed fee before any units are bought. The Regents often asks students to identify the y-intercept from a graph or to interpret its meaning in a real situation.

questions.prompt: The point (a, 0) where a graph crosses the x-axis, found by setting y=0 and solving for x.
questions.explanation: The x-intercept is also called the zero of the function. Worked example: for y=2x-8, set 0=2x-8, so x=4 — the x-intercept is (4, 0). NY F-IF.4 includes intercepts as key features. In context, an x-intercept might mean the time when a balance reaches zero or when an object lands. Regents items often present a graph and require both intercepts to be read off or computed from the given equation.

questions.prompt: The ratio (change in output)/(change in input) over an interval; equal to slope for linear functions but variable for nonlinear ones.
questions.explanation: Rate of change describes how y changes per unit x. Worked example: from (1, 5) to (4, 17) the rate is (17-5)/(4-1)=12/3=4 units of y per unit of x. NY F-IF.6 requires computing average rate of change over any interval. Linear functions have a constant rate (equal to slope), while quadratics and exponentials do not. Regents items use 'average rate of change' across an interval to compare growth — always divide change in y by change in x.

questions.prompt: The output of a function when the input is zero — the b in y=mx+b — representing the starting amount in real-world models.
questions.explanation: Initial value equals the y-intercept and the constant in slope-intercept form. Worked example: in y=4x+20, the initial value is 20 — at x=0 the output is 20. NY F-LE.2 and F-IF.7 cover this idea. In context: 'A pool starts with 20 gallons and fills at 4 gallons per minute, so V(t)=4t+20.' Regents items ask students to identify the initial value and explain what it represents in context — always include units.

questions.prompt: The linear equation form y=mx+b where m is slope and b is y-intercept, ideal for quick graphing and identifying key features.
questions.explanation: Slope-intercept form makes graphing easy. Worked example: y=2x-3 has slope 2 and y-intercept (0,-3); plot (0,-3), then rise 2 right 1 to (1,-1). NY F-IF.7 and F-LE.5 use this form. Students convert from standard or point-slope by isolating y. Regents items often ask students to rewrite 2x+3y=12 in slope-intercept form (y=(-2/3)x+4) to identify slope. The form is essential for comparing functions side-by-side.

questions.prompt: The linear equation form Ax+By=C where A, B, and C are integers and A is non-negative; useful for finding intercepts quickly.
questions.explanation: Standard form simplifies intercept finding. Worked example: in 3x+4y=12, set y=0 to find x=4, and set x=0 to find y=3 — intercepts (4,0) and (0,3). NY A-CED.2 uses standard form for context problems with two quantities. Regents items convert between standard and slope-intercept. Beware: -2x+3y=6 is sometimes rewritten with the leading coefficient made positive: 2x-3y=-6.

questions.prompt: The linear equation form y - y1 = m(x - x1) where (x1, y1) is a known point and m is the slope; useful when given a point and a slope.
questions.explanation: Point-slope is the form to use when a point and slope are given. Worked example: with slope 4 through (2, 7), the equation is y-7=4(x-2), which expands to y=4x-1. NY F-LE.2 includes writing equations from descriptions. Regents items often present a graph with a marked point and labeled slope; the fastest setup is point-slope, then convert to slope-intercept if the question demands it.

questions.prompt: A line of the form x=k with undefined slope, parallel to the y-axis, and not representing a function.
questions.explanation: Vertical lines have undefined slope (division by zero in rise/run) and are NOT functions because one x-value maps to infinitely many y-values. Worked example: x=5 is a vertical line through (5, 0), (5, 2), (5, -3), etc. NY F-IF.1 (function definition) is the relevant standard. Regents items use vertical-line failures to test the function definition; students should apply the vertical line test to determine whether a graph represents a function.

questions.prompt: A line of the form y=k with slope zero, parallel to the x-axis, representing a constant function.
questions.explanation: Horizontal lines have slope 0 because rise is zero. Worked example: y=4 passes through (0,4), (3,4), (-5,4); the slope is (4-4)/(any-any)=0. NY F-IF.4 includes constant functions. A horizontal line is a valid function (each x maps to one y). Regents items contrast horizontal (slope 0) with vertical (undefined slope) — a common trap is mixing the two. The y-intercept of y=4 is (0,4) itself.

questions.prompt: The input quantity, conventionally x, whose value is chosen freely and determines the output.
questions.explanation: The independent variable is the input. Worked example: in C=2t+5 modeling cost over time, t (time) is independent — you pick t, the equation produces C. NY F-IF.1 establishes this. On axes, the independent variable goes on the horizontal axis. Regents context items ask students to identify which variable is independent — typically the cause (time, items bought, hours worked) rather than the effect (cost, distance, total earnings).

questions.prompt: The output quantity, conventionally y, whose value depends on the input through the function rule.
questions.explanation: The dependent variable is the output. Worked example: in d=55t (distance equals 55 times hours), d depends on t — choose t, compute d. NY F-IF.1 makes this explicit. On axes, dependent goes on the vertical axis. Regents items ask students to label graphs correctly: when time is in hours and cost in dollars, time goes on x and cost goes on y because cost depends on time, not the reverse.

questions.prompt: The set of all permissible input values for a function, restricted by context and by algebraic undefined points.
questions.explanation: Domain is the input set. Worked example: f(x)=2x+1 has domain all real numbers; in context, if x represents seats sold, the domain is non-negative integers. NY F-IF.5 requires interpreting domain in context. Regents items often ask 'What is an appropriate domain?' for a real-world model — the answer is rarely 'all reals' because counting variables cannot be negative or fractional. State the bound and the type (integers).

questions.prompt: The set of all output values a function can produce, dependent on the domain and the function rule.
questions.explanation: Range is the output set. Worked example: f(x)=x^2 with domain all reals has range y is greater-than-or-equal 0 because squares are non-negative. NY F-IF.5 covers range interpretation. For linear functions over a restricted domain like 0 less-than-or-equal x less-than-or-equal 10, plug in the endpoints to find range bounds (e.g., y=2x+1 gives range 1 less-than-or-equal y less-than-or-equal 21). Regents items expect range stated with the correct inequality symbols and endpoint inclusion.

questions.prompt: A function whose output values grow as input values grow, equivalent to positive slope for a linear function.
questions.explanation: A function increases on an interval if for any x1 < x2 in the interval, f(x1) < f(x2). Worked example: y=3x+2 increases everywhere because slope 3 > 0. NY F-IF.4 uses 'increasing' and 'decreasing' to describe key features. Regents items show graphs and ask which interval is increasing; for parabolas, the graph increases on one side of the vertex and decreases on the other — always check direction relative to the vertex.

questions.prompt: A two-column listing of paired inputs and outputs, used to identify the constant difference that indicates a linear function.
questions.explanation: Tables reveal linearity through constant first differences in y when x changes by 1. Worked example: x=1,2,3,4 yields y=5,8,11,14 — differences 3,3,3, so the function is linear with slope 3 and y-intercept 2 (extrapolate back to x=0). NY F-IF.7 and F-LE.1 use tables to identify function type. Regents items present a table and ask students to write the equation; compute slope from any two points then back-solve for b.

questions.prompt: A straight-line plot of a function on the coordinate plane, with constant slope and one y-intercept.
questions.explanation: Linear graphs are non-vertical straight lines. Worked example: graph y=(1/2)x-3 by plotting (0,-3), moving right 2 and up 1 to (2,-2), and drawing the line. NY F-IF.7 requires graphing linear functions. Regents items show a graph and ask for the equation, or vice versa. Confirm linearity by checking that the slope is constant between any pair of marked points — if not, the graph is not linear.

questions.prompt: The shorthand f(x) for the output of function f at input x, replacing y in many algebraic settings.
questions.explanation: f(x) reads 'f of x' and equals the output. Worked example: if f(x)=2x+5, then f(3)=2(3)+5=11. NY F-IF.2 introduces this notation. Students must NOT interpret f(x) as f times x. Regents items use function notation in tables, equations, and contexts — 'find f(4)' means substitute 4 for x, while 'solve f(x)=10' means find x such that the output is 10. Distinguish input vs output questions.

questions.prompt: A function whose domain is the positive integers, often written as a_n or f(n), used for term-by-term linear or arithmetic patterns.
questions.explanation: Arithmetic sequences are linear functions with integer domain. Worked example: a_n=3n+1 gives 4, 7, 10, 13 ... — common difference 3, first term 4. NY F-IF.3 and F-LE.2 cover sequences. Regents items present the first few terms and ask for the n-th term formula; identify the common difference d (the slope) and the first term (used to find n=1 output). An arithmetic sequence can be rewritten as a_n=a_1+d(n-1) or a_n=dn+(a_1-d).

questions.prompt: A written-word description of a real-world linear relationship that students translate into an equation, table, or graph.
questions.explanation: Verbal models bridge words and algebra. Worked example: 'A printer costs $80 plus $0.05 per page' becomes C=0.05p+80, where p is pages. NY A-CED.2 and F-LE.5 require this translation. Regents items present a paragraph and ask students to write the equation, compute a specific value, and interpret slope or intercept. Highlight keywords: 'per' indicates rate (slope), 'starting' or 'flat fee' indicates initial value (y-intercept).

questions.prompt: Two lines in the same plane that share the same slope but different y-intercepts and therefore never intersect.
questions.explanation: Parallel lines have equal slopes. Worked example: y=2x+3 and y=2x-5 are parallel — both slope 2, never intersect. NY G-GPE.5 in geometry connects to Algebra 1 slope work. The system formed by two parallel lines has no solution (consistent with the 'no solution' system case). Regents items often ask for the equation of a line parallel to a given one through a specified point — use the given slope and point-slope form.

questions.prompt: Two lines whose slopes are negative reciprocals, meeting at a 90-degree angle and producing a product of slopes equal to -1.
questions.explanation: Perpendicular slopes multiply to -1. Worked example: y=2x+1 and y=(-1/2)x+4 are perpendicular because 2 * (-1/2) = -1. NY G-GPE.5 introduces this in algebra context. To find a perpendicular line through a point, flip the slope and change its sign, then use point-slope form. Regents items frequently test this — a common wrong answer uses only the reciprocal without changing sign or only the sign without reciprocal.

questions.prompt: A change that occurs by the same amount per unit input across the entire domain, distinguishing linear from nonlinear behavior.
questions.explanation: Constant rate is the defining property of linear functions. Worked example: if a tree grows 2 inches per month every month, the height is linear in time, h=2t+initial. NY F-LE.1 requires distinguishing constant from changing rates. Regents items test this with tables — if first differences are constant, the function is linear; if second differences are constant, the function is quadratic; if ratios are constant, the function is exponential.

questions.prompt: The (x, y) coordinate where two lines meet, representing the simultaneous solution to both equations.
questions.explanation: Intersection of two lines is the system's solution. Worked example: y=x+1 and y=-x+5 intersect at (2, 3) — set x+1=-x+5 to get 2x=4 and x=2, then y=3. NY A-REI.6 covers systems. Regents items may give a graph with a clearly marked intersection or require algebraic solving. The intersection point is the unique ordered pair that satisfies both equations simultaneously.

questions.prompt: The statistical method of finding the line of best fit y=mx+b that minimizes the sum of squared residuals for a scatter plot.
questions.explanation: Linear regression produces a model for noisy data. Worked example: a calculator's LinReg on points (1,3), (2,5), (3,6.5) yields y approximately 1.75x+1.33. NY S-ID.6 requires computing and interpreting linear models. Regents items provide bivariate data and ask students to use a calculator's regression feature, then interpret slope and intercept in context. The correlation coefficient r (close to plus/minus 1 means strong) accompanies the regression.

questions.prompt: The ordered pair (x, y) where the graphs of two equations meet, which is simultaneously a solution to both equations.
questions.explanation: Intersection points solve systems graphically. Worked example: y=x+1 and y=-2x+7 intersect at (2, 3); substitute to check: 3=2+1 (true) and 3=-2(2)+7 (true). NY A-REI.6 requires this. Regents items show two lines on the same grid and ask students to read or verify the intersection. The intersection represents a context value where both quantities are equal — like the moment two cars are at the same distance from a starting point.

questions.prompt: Any (x, y) coordinate that satisfies every equation in the system when substituted; for two linear equations this is usually one unique point.
questions.explanation: An ordered pair must satisfy ALL equations in the system. Worked example: is (3, 4) a solution of x+y=7 and 2x-y=2? Check: 3+4=7 (true) and 2(3)-4=2 (true), so yes. NY A-REI.6 covers checking. Regents Part I items list ordered pairs and ask which is the solution; substitute each candidate into both equations and discard any that fail one. The check must succeed on both, not just one.

questions.prompt: The outcome when two lines have different slopes and intersect at exactly one point, giving the system a unique ordered-pair solution.
questions.explanation: A system with one solution has two lines crossing once. Worked example: y=2x+1 and y=-x+4 have different slopes (2 and -1), so they intersect once — at (1, 3). NY A-REI.6 covers all three system outcomes. Regents items ask 'How many solutions does the system have?' Determining different slopes algebraically (after converting to y=mx+b) is the fastest method without graphing the lines explicitly.

questions.prompt: The outcome when two lines have the same slope but different y-intercepts (parallel lines) and never intersect.
questions.explanation: No solution corresponds to parallel lines. Worked example: y=2x+3 and y=2x-5 both have slope 2 but different intercepts; substituting yields 2x+3=2x-5, simplifying to 3=-5 (false), so no solution. NY A-REI.6 covers this. Regents items test 'no solution' as one of three possible answers; verify by either graphing (parallel lines) or solving algebraically and reaching a false statement.

questions.prompt: The outcome when two equations describe the same line; every point on the line satisfies both, so the system has unlimited solutions.
questions.explanation: Infinitely many solutions means the equations are equivalent. Worked example: y=2x+3 and 2y=4x+6 are the same line (divide the second by 2 to recover the first). Substitution yields 0=0, which is always true. NY A-REI.6 covers this. Regents items disguise this with multiplied versions of the same equation; reducing both to slope-intercept form and seeing identical results confirms infinitely many solutions.

questions.prompt: The algebraic technique of solving one equation for a variable, then substituting that expression into the other equation to solve.
questions.explanation: Substitution works best when one equation already has an isolated variable. Worked example: solve y=2x+1 and 3x+y=11 by substituting 2x+1 for y: 3x+(2x+1)=11, so 5x=10 and x=2, giving y=5. NY A-REI.6 requires this method. Regents items often include one equation already in y=form, signaling substitution. Always back-substitute to find the second variable and verify the answer in both originals.

questions.prompt: The algebraic technique of adding or subtracting equations to eliminate one variable, often after multiplying to create matching coefficients.
questions.explanation: Elimination cancels a variable via addition. Worked example: 2x+y=10 and 3x-y=5; add to get 5x=15, so x=3, then y=4. NY A-REI.6 requires fluency. Sometimes multiplication is needed: for x+2y=5 and 3x+y=10, multiply the first by 3 to get 3x+6y=15, then subtract from 3x+y=10 to get -5y=-5, so y=1. Regents items award full credit for either substitution or elimination as long as work is shown.

questions.prompt: The visual technique of plotting both equations on the same coordinate plane and identifying their intersection point as the solution.
questions.explanation: Graphing is reliable when intersections are at integer coordinates. Worked example: plot y=x+1 and y=-x+5 on a grid; they meet at (2, 3). NY A-REI.6 and A-REI.11 cover graphical solving. Regents items often include a coordinate grid in the test booklet for this purpose. Graphing fails when intersections are non-integer — always verify by substituting the read coordinates into both equations algebraically.

questions.prompt: A coefficient-only rectangular array that organizes a system's numerical data, supporting elimination via row operations on a calculator.
questions.explanation: Matrices store coefficients compactly. Worked example: the system 2x+y=5 and x-y=1 has augmented matrix [[2,1,5],[1,-1,1]]. NY does not require matrix algebra in Algebra 1, but TI calculators include rref (reduced row echelon form) that returns the solution directly. Regents items rarely require matrices, but advanced students may use them as a check on substitution or elimination work.

questions.prompt: The verification step of substituting both x and y values back into both original equations to confirm the proposed solution.
questions.explanation: Checking confirms accuracy. Worked example: claimed solution (3, 2) for 2x+y=8 and x-y=1; check: 2(3)+2=8 (true) and 3-2=1 (true), confirmed. NY A-REI.6 expects verification. Regents 2-point items often award one point for correct answer and one for the check. A common error: checking only one equation — both must be verified because the ordered pair must satisfy the entire system simultaneously.

questions.prompt: The condition where two linear equations have identical slope values, producing either parallel lines (no solution) or the same line (infinite).
questions.explanation: Same slope means the lines never cross or are identical. Worked example: y=3x+2 and y=3x-1 both have slope 3 but different intercepts — parallel, no solution. y=3x+2 and 2y=6x+4 also have slope 3 but identical intercepts (after simplifying), so infinite solutions. NY A-REI.6 covers both subcases. Regents items present both equations in different forms — convert each to slope-intercept and compare m and b.

questions.prompt: The condition where two linear equations have unequal slopes, guaranteeing exactly one intersection point regardless of intercept values.
questions.explanation: Different slopes mean the lines must cross once. Worked example: y=2x+1 (slope 2) and y=-3x+8 (slope -3) intersect at exactly one point. NY A-REI.6 confirms this. Regents items use this to test the 'how many solutions' question — different slopes guarantees one solution. Finding the intersection still requires substitution, elimination, or graphing; knowing it exists is the first step.

questions.prompt: The condition where two equations are equivalent (one is a multiple of the other), producing the identical graph and infinite solutions.
questions.explanation: Same line means infinite solutions. Worked example: x+y=4 and 2x+2y=8 are the same line — divide the second by 2 to recover the first. Algebraically the system reduces to 0=0. NY A-REI.6 covers this. Regents items hide this by scaling both sides; students should reduce both equations to lowest terms and compare. A common error: assuming different-looking equations describe different lines without simplifying first.

questions.prompt: Two lines with the same slope but different y-intercepts, producing zero intersection points and a system with no solution.
questions.explanation: Parallel lines never cross. Worked example: y=4x-1 and y=4x+6 are parallel (same slope 4, different intercepts) — system has no solution. Confirm algebraically: 4x-1=4x+6 simplifies to -1=6 (false). NY A-REI.6 connects parallel lines to systems. Regents items often graph parallel lines and ask students to identify how many solutions; the answer is zero, not one or infinite.

questions.prompt: A system of equations that has at least one solution — either exactly one (independent) or infinitely many (dependent).
questions.explanation: Consistent systems have at least one solution. Worked example: y=x+1 and y=-x+5 is consistent with one solution (2, 3). y=2x+3 and 2y=4x+6 is consistent with infinitely many. The opposite, inconsistent, has no solution (parallel lines). NY A-REI.6 uses this vocabulary occasionally. Regents items may ask students to classify a system as consistent/inconsistent and dependent/independent — three categories combined into two answers per system.

questions.prompt: A context where two ingredients with different concentrations or prices are combined; system equations track total amount and total quality.
questions.explanation: Mixture problems use one equation for total quantity and one for total value/concentration. Worked example: mix 10% acid with 40% acid to get 100 mL of 25% — x+y=100 and 0.10x+0.40y=25 yields x=50, y=50. NY A-CED.2 and A-REI.6 cover modeling and solving. Regents items present coffee, alcohol, candy, or salt-water mixtures; identify the two unknowns clearly (mL of solution A and B) before writing equations.

questions.prompt: A context with two ticket prices (adult, child) sold in two equations — one for total tickets, one for total revenue — solved as a system.
questions.explanation: Ticket problems use number-and-value equations. Worked example: adult tickets $12, child tickets $7, total 50 tickets sold for $475 — a+c=50 and 12a+7c=475 yields a=25, c=25. NY A-CED.2 requires writing the system; A-REI.6 requires solving. Regents items vary the context (movie tickets, concert seats, museum admission); the structure is identical — count equation plus price equation.

questions.prompt: A system where cost and revenue equations are set equal to find the production level at which profit equals zero.
questions.explanation: Break-even is where revenue equals cost. Worked example: cost C=200+5x, revenue R=15x; set R=C to get 15x=200+5x, so 10x=200 and x=20 — selling 20 units breaks even. NY A-CED.3 and A-REI.6 cover modeling. Regents items often ask 'How many items must be sold to break even?' Below break-even is loss, above is profit. Always state units (items, hours, units sold) in the answer.

questions.prompt: A real-world limit (capacity, budget, time) written as a linear inequality alongside other equations or inequalities in a system.
questions.explanation: Constraints filter feasible solutions. Worked example: 'spend at most $40 on apples ($2 each) and bread ($3 each)' becomes 2a+3b is less-than-or-equal 40, plus a is greater-than-or-equal 0 and b is greater-than-or-equal 0. NY A-CED.3 requires representing constraints. Regents modeling items list multiple restrictions; each translates into a separate inequality, and the solution set is the intersection (feasible region) of all of them.

questions.prompt: The set of (x, y) points satisfying every inequality in a system; on a graph, the overlapping shaded region where all constraints hold.
questions.explanation: The feasible region is the intersection of shaded half-planes. Worked example: for x+y is less-than-or-equal 6, x is greater-than-or-equal 0, y is greater-than-or-equal 0, the feasible region is the triangular shaded area with vertices (0,0), (6,0), (0,6). NY A-REI.12 covers graphing systems of linear inequalities. Regents items present a region and ask which ordered pair lies inside, on the boundary, or outside — substitute the candidate into each inequality.

questions.prompt: The visual representation of an inequality like y<2x+3 as a shaded half-plane bounded by the corresponding line.
questions.explanation: Graphing inequalities requires the boundary plus shading. Worked example: y<2x+3 is graphed with a dashed line through (0,3) with slope 2; shade BELOW the line. NY A-REI.12 requires this. Use solid line for less-than-or-equal/greater-than-or-equal, dashed for strict < or >. Regents items present the graph and ask for the inequality, or vice versa; verify shading direction with a test point like (0,0) substituted into the inequality.

questions.prompt: The portion of the coordinate plane on one side of a boundary line that contains all the (x, y) values satisfying a linear inequality.
questions.explanation: Half-planes are the solution sets of strict and non-strict linear inequalities. Worked example: y is greater-than-or-equal x-2 shades the half-plane above the solid line y=x-2. NY A-REI.12 covers this. To decide which side, pick a test point not on the line (often (0,0)) and substitute; if the inequality is true, shade that side. Regents items penalize wrong-side shading and using the wrong boundary type.

questions.prompt: The line obtained by replacing the inequality symbol with an equals sign, drawn solid for less-than-or-equal/greater-than-or-equal and dashed for < or >.
questions.explanation: The boundary separates solution from non-solution. Worked example: for y<3x+1, the boundary line is y=3x+1 (dashed because strict). For y is less-than-or-equal 3x+1, the boundary is solid (included). NY A-REI.12 specifies this convention. Regents items provide a graph with dashed or solid boundaries; students must match symbol type — a dashed line with shading above might be y>3x+1, never y is greater-than-or-equal 3x+1.

questions.prompt: The intersection of two or more shaded half-planes, representing the solution set of a system of linear inequalities.
questions.explanation: Overlap is the simultaneous solution of all constraints. Worked example: y<x+2 and y is greater-than-or-equal -x-1 produce two half-planes; the overlap (darker double-shaded region) is the system's solution. NY A-REI.12 requires graphing the system. Regents items often shade the final region with a different pattern (cross-hatching, darker shading). Any point in the overlap satisfies every inequality; any point outside fails at least one.

questions.prompt: A specific coordinate substituted into an inequality to decide which half-plane to shade, often (0,0) if the line does not pass through the origin.
questions.explanation: Test points determine shading direction. Worked example: for 2x+3y<6, test (0,0): 2(0)+3(0)=0<6 (true), so shade the side containing the origin. NY A-REI.12 implicitly uses this. Regents items rely on this strategy. If the boundary passes through (0,0), pick another easy point like (1,0) or (0,1). A common error: forgetting to test and shading the wrong side based on visual intuition.

questions.prompt: The exponent law stating that x^a times x^b equals x^(a+b) when the bases are identical, used to simplify multiplied powers.
questions.explanation: The product rule combines like bases. Worked example: x^3 times x^5 = x^(3+5) = x^8. NY 8.EE.1 introduces this; A-SSE.2 extends it to algebraic manipulation. Worked example with coefficients: 2x^3 * 4x^2 = 8x^5. Regents items often combine this with the power rule. Common error: multiplying the exponents instead of adding — 3+5=8 (add), not 15 (multiply). The bases must be identical for the rule to apply.

questions.prompt: The exponent law stating that x^a divided by x^b equals x^(a-b) when the bases match, used to simplify fractions of powers.
questions.explanation: Quotient rule subtracts exponents. Worked example: x^7/x^3 = x^(7-3) = x^4. With coefficients: 12x^5/4x^2 = 3x^3. NY 8.EE.1 covers this. The rule explains why x^0 = 1: x^5/x^5 = x^0 must equal 1. Regents items frequently test this in simplification problems. Common error: subtracting the wrong way (3-7 instead of 7-3) — the numerator's exponent always comes first in the subtraction.

questions.prompt: The exponent law stating that (x^a)^b equals x^(a*b), used when a power is raised to another power.
questions.explanation: Power rule multiplies exponents. Worked example: (x^3)^4 = x^(3*4) = x^12. NY 8.EE.1 covers this. With coefficients: (2x^3)^4 = 2^4 * x^(3*4) = 16x^12 — the exponent applies to BOTH the coefficient and the variable. Regents items combine this with other exponent rules. Common error: applying the exponent only to the variable, forgetting to raise the coefficient (writing 2x^12 instead of 16x^12).

questions.prompt: The convention that any nonzero base raised to the zero power equals 1, derived from the quotient rule applied to identical exponents.
questions.explanation: Zero exponent gives 1. Worked example: 5^0 = 1, (3x)^0 = 1, but 0^0 is undefined. NY 8.EE.1 establishes this. The convention is consistent: x^5/x^5 = x^0 by the quotient rule and equals 1 by direct division. Regents items use this to test understanding; many Part I distractors set the answer to 0. Note: 0 * x^0 = 0 (because of the leading coefficient), not 1 — the zero-exponent rule applies only to the power, not coefficients.

questions.prompt: The convention that x^(-n) equals 1/x^n, allowing negative exponents to be rewritten as positive exponents in the denominator.
questions.explanation: Negative exponents move the base to the denominator. Worked example: 2^(-3) = 1/2^3 = 1/8, and x^(-4) = 1/x^4. NY 8.EE.1 introduces this. With multiplication: 5x^(-2) = 5/x^2. Regents items combine negative exponents with other rules; simplify all negatives last so they don't compound. Common error: making the entire fraction negative (writing -1/x^4) instead of moving the base — the value 1/x^4 is positive when x is.

questions.prompt: A number expressed as a * 10^n where 1 is less-than-or-equal |a| < 10 and n is an integer, used for very large or very small magnitudes.
questions.explanation: Scientific notation compresses extreme values. Worked example: 3,400,000 = 3.4 * 10^6 and 0.0000072 = 7.2 * 10^-6. NY 8.EE.3-4 requires writing and operating with scientific notation. The mantissa a must satisfy 1 is less-than-or-equal |a| < 10 (so 34 * 10^5 is not in scientific notation; rewrite as 3.4 * 10^6). Regents items test conversion both directions and arithmetic operations with scientific notation.

questions.prompt: A number written out fully with all digits and decimal places, the opposite of scientific notation.
questions.explanation: Standard notation spells out the value. Worked example: 4.5 * 10^4 in standard notation is 45,000. 6.2 * 10^-3 is 0.0062. NY 8.EE.4 includes conversion. Move the decimal point n places right for positive exponent, left for negative. Regents items provide scientific notation and ask for the standard form, or vice versa — count zeros carefully. Common error: confusing direction for negative exponents (decimal moves left, value gets smaller).

questions.prompt: The integer power of 10 that represents the approximate size of a number, used to compare numerical scales like 10^3 vs 10^6.
questions.explanation: Order of magnitude compares scale. Worked example: 4.2 * 10^7 (ten million) is three orders of magnitude larger than 5.1 * 10^4 (fifty thousand) because 10^7 / 10^4 = 10^3 = 1000 times bigger. NY 8.EE.3 covers this comparison. Regents items present two scientific-notation values and ask 'How many times larger is X than Y?' — subtract exponents to get the order-of-magnitude difference.

questions.prompt: Numbers of the form 10^n that form the backbone of scientific notation, the metric system, and place-value reasoning.
questions.explanation: Powers of ten are 10, 100, 1000 ... and 0.1, 0.01, 0.001. Worked example: 10^3 = 1000 (three zeros), 10^-2 = 0.01 (two decimal places). NY 8.EE.1 covers exponent rules with base 10. Each power of ten shifts the decimal one place. Regents items test arithmetic with powers of 10 (multiplying 4 * 10^5 by 3 * 10^2 equals 12 * 10^7 = 1.2 * 10^8). Always renormalize the mantissa to be at least 1 and less than 10.

questions.prompt: The display format like 3.5E8 or 3.5 EE 8 used by graphing calculators to show scientific notation when results exceed the screen width.
questions.explanation: Calculator notation uses E for exponent. Worked example: 3.5E8 on a TI-84 display means 3.5 * 10^8 = 350,000,000. NY does not formally test calculator notation, but Regents calculators (TI-30XS, TI-84) display this format. Students must translate to a * 10^n form before writing their answer. Common error: copying '3.5E8' as a final answer; convert to scientific or standard notation according to the question's instructions.

questions.prompt: The number that, when multiplied by itself, gives the radicand; sqrt(25) = 5 because 5 * 5 = 25.
questions.explanation: Square root is the principal (positive) value. Worked example: sqrt(49) = 7, sqrt(144) = 12, sqrt(20) = sqrt(4*5) = 2*sqrt(5). NY N-RN.2 covers radical operations. The principal root convention excludes the negative root; if the equation x^2=25 is solved, the answer is x = plus or minus 5, but sqrt(25) alone is 5. Regents items test perfect squares (1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, 121, 144, 169, 196, 225) — memorize these.

questions.prompt: The number that, when cubed, gives the radicand; cbrt(8) = 2 because 2 * 2 * 2 = 8.
questions.explanation: Cube roots can be negative because (-2)^3 = -8. Worked example: cbrt(27) = 3, cbrt(-64) = -4 (since (-4)^3=-64), cbrt(125) = 5. NY N-RN.2 covers cube roots. Perfect cubes: 1, 8, 27, 64, 125, 216, 343. Regents items sometimes embed cube roots in volume problems (cube root of volume gives side length). Unlike square roots, cube roots accept negative radicands.

questions.prompt: The quantity sitting under the radical sign — for sqrt(x+5), the radicand is x+5.
questions.explanation: Radicand is the expression inside the radical. Worked example: in sqrt(2x+8), the radicand is 2x+8 and must be greater-than-or-equal 0 for real-number outputs, so x is greater-than-or-equal -4. NY A-REI.2 covers radical equations. Regents items use radicand to test domain understanding. To solve sqrt(2x+8)=4, square both sides to get 2x+8=16, then x=4 — and check (the radicand must be non-negative and the original equation must hold).

questions.prompt: A radical expression with no perfect-square factors under the root, no fractions inside the radical, and no radicals in the denominator.
questions.explanation: Simplest radical form factors out perfect squares. Worked example: sqrt(72) = sqrt(36*2) = 6*sqrt(2). For sqrt(50): factor 50 = 25*2, so sqrt(50) = 5*sqrt(2). NY N-RN.2 requires simplifying to standard form. Regents items present radicals and ask for the simplest form. Common error: stopping too early — sqrt(8) is not simplified; rewrite as 2*sqrt(2). Always check whether 4, 9, 16, 25 divide the radicand.

questions.prompt: A whole number that is the square of an integer, like 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100, and so on.
questions.explanation: Perfect squares have integer square roots. Worked example: 144 is a perfect square because sqrt(144) = 12. Variable perfect squares include x^2, 4x^2, 25x^2. NY N-RN.2 and A-SSE.2 use perfect squares for simplification and factoring (difference of squares). Regents items use perfect squares for radical simplification — extract them from larger radicands. Memorize squares from 1 to 25: 1, 4, 9, ..., 625.

questions.prompt: An exponent written as a fraction, like x^(1/2) or x^(2/3), where the denominator indicates a root and the numerator indicates a power.
questions.explanation: Fractional exponents are radicals. Worked example: x^(1/2) = sqrt(x), x^(1/3) = cbrt(x), x^(2/3) = cbrt(x^2) or (cbrt(x))^2. NY N-RN.1-2 cover this conversion. The denominator is always the root index. Regents items rewrite radicals as rational exponents and vice versa. Common evaluation: 8^(2/3) = (cbrt(8))^2 = 2^2 = 4 — take the cube root first (smaller numbers) then square.

questions.prompt: The small number written just outside the radical sign indicating which root to take; index 2 is a square root, index 3 is a cube root.
questions.explanation: The index specifies the root type. Worked example: in cbrt(x) the index is 3; in sqrt(x) the index is 2 (usually unwritten). NY N-RN.1 introduces this notation. Index 4 gives fourth roots: 4th-root(16) = 2 because 2^4 = 16. Regents items rarely use indices above 3 explicitly but rational exponents like x^(1/4) are equivalent. The index is the denominator in fractional-exponent form: x^(1/n) = nth-root(x).

questions.prompt: The non-negative root convention used for square roots (and even-index roots) so that the radical function returns a single value.
questions.explanation: Principal root is the standard positive choice. Worked example: sqrt(36) = 6 (principal), not -6 — even though (-6)^2 = 36. NY N-RN.2 follows this convention. Regents items use sqrt without ambiguity to mean the principal root. The equation x^2 = 36 has two solutions (plus and minus 6), but the function sqrt(36) returns only 6. Cube roots (and odd indices) have no ambiguity — cbrt(8) = 2 only.

questions.prompt: An algebraic rewriting of a radical or exponential expression that uses different notation but represents the same value.
questions.explanation: Equivalent forms swap radicals and rational exponents. Worked example: sqrt(x^3) and x^(3/2) are equivalent; cbrt(x^4) and x^(4/3) are equivalent. NY N-RN.1-2 expects students to convert in both directions. Regents items present an expression and ask which choice is equivalent. Always check by substituting a simple value like x=4 into both forms — equivalent expressions must produce identical numerical outputs.

questions.prompt: The bottom number in a fractional exponent, which determines the root index: in x^(2/5), the denominator 5 means the fifth root.
questions.explanation: Denominator equals root index. Worked example: x^(3/4) = 4th-root(x^3). NY N-RN.1 makes this connection. For 27^(2/3): denominator 3 means cube root, numerator 2 means square the result — cbrt(27)=3, then 3^2 = 9. Regents items often test this with perfect-power radicands (8, 27, 64, 125). Compute the root first to keep numbers manageable, then apply the power.

questions.prompt: The skill of producing an approximate value for a complex calculation by rounding to convenient numbers before computing.
questions.explanation: Estimation checks reasonableness. Worked example: estimate 3.97 * 198 by rounding to 4 * 200 = 800; exact value 786.06 confirms the estimate. NY 7.NS.3 and 8.EE.4 build estimation. Regents items embed estimation in reasonableness checks — after computing 4.7E6, ask 'Is the answer about 5 million?' Estimation also helps reject calculator-typing errors when the final value differs from a quick mental check by a factor of 10.

questions.prompt: A ratio that compares corresponding dimensions of two similar figures or two quantities, equal to new value divided by original value.
questions.explanation: Scale factor is a multiplicative ratio. Worked example: if a triangle's sides scale from (3,4,5) to (9,12,15), the scale factor is 3. NY 7.G.1 introduces scale; A-CED.2 uses scale in modeling (growth/decay factors are scale factors). Regents items use scale in geometry and exponential models. Doubling has scale factor 2; halving has scale factor 1/2 or 0.5. The scale factor multiplies the original to produce the scaled value.

questions.prompt: Multiplying by a conversion factor (a ratio equal to 1) to change a measurement from one unit to another, like inches to centimeters.
questions.explanation: Unit conversion uses dimensional analysis. Worked example: 5 feet to inches — multiply by 12 in/ft to get 60 inches. To convert 100 km/h to m/s: 100 km/h * 1000 m/km * 1 h/3600 s = 27.78 m/s. NY N-Q.1 requires fluency with units. Regents items often involve speed (mph to ft/s), area (sq ft to sq m), or money rates. Always cancel units carefully and label final answers with the correct unit.

questions.prompt: The multiplier (1+r) applied each period in an exponential growth model, where r is the decimal growth rate.
questions.explanation: Growth factor multiplies the previous value. Worked example: a 5% annual growth has growth factor 1.05; a population of 1000 after 3 years is 1000 * 1.05^3 = 1157.625. NY F-LE.1 and A-SSE.3 cover growth. The general form is A = A_0 * b^t where b > 1 for growth. Regents items test interest, population, and bacterial growth — find r from the factor (b-1) and interpret in context (e.g., 1.07 means 7% growth per period).

questions.prompt: The multiplier (1-r) applied each period in an exponential decay model, where r is the decimal decay rate, producing a factor between 0 and 1.
questions.explanation: Decay factor is less than 1. Worked example: 10% annual decline has decay factor 0.90; a $10,000 car worth 10000 * 0.90^5 after 5 years is about $5905. NY F-LE.1 and A-SSE.3 cover this. The general form A = A_0 * b^t with 0 < b < 1 is decay. Regents items test depreciation, radioactive decay, and medicine clearance — find r from the factor (1-b) and interpret as percent decline per period.

questions.prompt: A polynomial with exactly one term, such as 5x^2, -7, or 3xy.
questions.explanation: Monomials have one term and non-negative integer exponents. Worked examples: 4x, -7, 3x^2y, and 9 are all monomials. NY A-APR.1 introduces polynomial vocabulary. The degree of a monomial is the sum of exponents on its variables: 3x^2y has degree 3. Regents items use monomial vocabulary in classification questions and in factoring (GCF is always a monomial). The coefficient and degree together describe each monomial.

questions.prompt: A polynomial with exactly two terms separated by + or -, such as x^2-9 or 3x+5.
questions.explanation: Binomials have two terms. Worked examples: x+5, 2x^2-7x, and a^2-b^2 are binomials. NY A-APR.1 and A-SSE.2 use binomials extensively. Multiplying two binomials uses FOIL: (x+3)(x-2) = x^2+x-6. The Regents tests binomials in factoring (difference of squares: a^2-b^2=(a+b)(a-b)) and in multiplication. Trinomials and higher-term polynomials build from binomial operations.

questions.prompt: A polynomial with exactly three terms separated by + or -, such as x^2+5x+6.
questions.explanation: Trinomials have three terms. Worked examples: x^2+5x+6, 2a^2-3a+1, and y^2-9y+20 are trinomials. NY A-SSE.2-3 includes factoring quadratic trinomials. Standard-form quadratic trinomials ax^2+bx+c are central to Unit 5-6. Factoring x^2+5x+6 = (x+2)(x+3) requires finding two numbers that multiply to c and add to b. Regents items frequently test trinomial factoring as a 2-point Part II item or as multiple-choice Part I.

questions.prompt: The largest exponent on a variable in a polynomial — for 4x^3-2x^2+x-5, the degree is 3.
questions.explanation: Degree determines polynomial behavior. Worked examples: x^4-3x has degree 4; 2x^3+x^2-7 has degree 3 (cubic); 5x^2+3 has degree 2 (quadratic). NY A-APR.1 introduces degree. The degree predicts the number of solutions (real or complex) for a polynomial equation — degree 2 has up to 2 solutions, degree 3 has up to 3, etc. Regents Algebra 1 focuses on degree 1 (linear) and degree 2 (quadratic) but expects students to recognize higher degrees.

questions.prompt: The numerical coefficient of the highest-degree term when a polynomial is written in standard form (descending powers).
questions.explanation: Leading coefficient is the front number in standard form. Worked examples: in 3x^2-5x+1, the leading coefficient is 3; in -4x^3+x-7, it is -4. NY A-APR.1 references this. The sign of the leading coefficient for a quadratic determines whether the parabola opens up (positive) or down (negative). Regents items use this to interpret quadratic graphs and to test standard-form recognition.

questions.prompt: Combining two polynomials by grouping like terms — terms with the same variable raised to the same power — and adding their coefficients.
questions.explanation: Add like terms. Worked example: (3x^2+5x-2)+(x^2-4x+7) = 4x^2+x+5. NY A-APR.1 covers this. Align terms by degree before adding. Regents items often present this as a Part I (multiple choice) or Part II (show work) item. Common error: combining unlike terms — 3x^2 cannot combine with 5x because the exponents differ. Use parentheses to track signs cleanly when adding.

questions.prompt: Subtracting one polynomial from another by distributing the negative sign and then combining like terms.
questions.explanation: Distribute the minus sign before combining. Worked example: (5x^2-3x+1) - (2x^2-7x+4) = 5x^2-3x+1-2x^2+7x-4 = 3x^2+4x-3. NY A-APR.1 covers this. The sign change applies to EVERY term in the subtrahend, not just the first one. Regents items penalize sign errors — write the subtraction with explicit parentheses, distribute the negative, then collect like terms.

questions.prompt: Combining two polynomials by distributing each term in one across every term in the other, then combining like terms in the result.
questions.explanation: Distribute every term. Worked example: (x+3)(x^2-2x+5) = x^3-2x^2+5x+3x^2-6x+15 = x^3+x^2-x+15. NY A-APR.1 covers this. For trinomial-by-trinomial, use a 3-by-3 grid (box method) to organize products. Regents items test binomial-by-binomial (FOIL) most often, with occasional binomial-by-trinomial. Be systematic: every term in the first must multiply every term in the second.

questions.prompt: A mnemonic for multiplying two binomials by computing First, Outer, Inner, Last products and adding them.
questions.explanation: FOIL is shorthand for the distributive property on binomials. Worked example: (x+4)(x-3): First x*x = x^2, Outer x*(-3) = -3x, Inner 4*x = 4x, Last 4*(-3) = -12. Sum: x^2+x-12. NY A-APR.1 implicitly covers FOIL. Regents Part I items test FOIL fluency. Common error: only computing F and L (forgetting Outer and Inner) — this misses the linear term. Use a box-grid alternative for students who prefer it.

questions.prompt: Two or more terms with identical variable parts and exponents that can be combined by adding or subtracting coefficients.
questions.explanation: Like terms share variables and powers. Worked examples: 3x^2 and 7x^2 are like; 4ab and -2ab are like; 3x^2 and 3x are NOT like. NY A-APR.1 uses this concept. To combine: 5x^3-2x^3 = 3x^3 (subtract coefficients, keep variable). Regents items embed like-terms reasoning in polynomial operations. Color-coding or underlining matching variable parts helps prevent miscombining.

questions.prompt: The largest monomial that divides evenly into every term of a polynomial, factored out as the first step in most factoring problems.
questions.explanation: GCF is the first factoring step. Worked example: 12x^3-8x^2+4x = 4x(3x^2-2x+1) — factor out 4x (the GCF). NY A-SSE.2 requires identifying and factoring GCFs. Find the GCF by taking the smallest power of each variable common to all terms and the GCD of coefficients. Regents items penalize incomplete factoring — after a GCF is removed, check whether the remaining polynomial can be factored further (often a difference of squares or trinomial).

questions.prompt: The factoring pattern a^2 - b^2 = (a+b)(a-b), recognized by two perfect-square terms separated by a minus sign.
questions.explanation: Difference of squares factors into conjugate pairs. Worked examples: x^2-25 = (x+5)(x-5); 4x^2-9 = (2x+3)(2x-3); x^2-49 = (x+7)(x-7). NY A-SSE.2 names this pattern. The two terms must be perfect squares and the operation between them MUST be subtraction. Regents items test this pattern in Part I. Sum of squares like x^2+25 does NOT factor over the reals.

questions.prompt: A trinomial of the form a^2 + 2ab + b^2 that factors as (a+b)^2, or a^2 - 2ab + b^2 that factors as (a-b)^2.
questions.explanation: Perfect-square trinomials have a structure of square + double product + square. Worked example: x^2+10x+25 = (x+5)^2 because 5+5=10 (the middle) and 5*5=25 (the last). NY A-SSE.2 names this. Recognize by checking that the first and last terms are perfect squares and the middle is twice the product of their roots. Regents items use perfect-square trinomials in factoring and in completing the square (Unit 6).

questions.prompt: A polynomial expressed as a product of factors, equivalent to standard form but useful for finding zeros and graphing.
questions.explanation: Factored form exposes the zeros. Worked example: x^2-5x+6 in factored form is (x-2)(x-3); setting each factor to zero gives x=2 and x=3 as zeros. NY A-SSE.3 connects factored form to graph features. The Regents asks students to convert between factored and standard forms and to identify x-intercepts of quadratics directly from factored form using the Zero Product Property.

questions.prompt: A repeated binomial that appears in both groups of a 4-term polynomial, factored out using grouping.
questions.explanation: Grouping pulls out a common binomial. Worked example: x^3+2x^2+3x+6 — group as x^2(x+2)+3(x+2), then factor (x+2) to get (x+2)(x^2+3). NY A-SSE.2 uses grouping for 4-term polynomials and for the AC method on hard trinomials. Regents items occasionally test grouping. The key is recognizing the matching binomial in both groups — if it does not match, try rearranging terms before grouping.

questions.prompt: The principle that if a product AB = 0, then A=0 or B=0 (or both), used to solve factored quadratic equations.
questions.explanation: Zero product turns factoring into solving. Worked example: (x-3)(x+2) = 0 implies x-3=0 or x+2=0, so x=3 or x=-2. NY A-REI.4 explicitly cites this property. After factoring a quadratic equation, apply ZPP to each factor and solve. Regents items often factor a quadratic in one problem and apply ZPP in the next. Common error: applying ZPP when the equation does not equal zero — always move everything to one side first.

questions.prompt: A solution to a polynomial equation; for quadratic ax^2+bx+c=0, the roots are also the x-intercepts of the corresponding graph.
questions.explanation: Roots are values that make the polynomial zero. Worked example: the roots of x^2-5x+6=0 are x=2 and x=3 (from factored form (x-2)(x-3)=0). NY A-APR.3 connects roots to graphs. A quadratic has at most 2 real roots; the Discriminant b^2-4ac determines how many. Regents items use the words 'roots', 'zeros', 'solutions', and 'x-intercepts' interchangeably for the same set of values.

questions.prompt: The polynomial form ax^2+bx+c with terms ordered by descending powers, where a is non-zero for a quadratic.
questions.explanation: Standard form orders terms descending. Worked example: rewrite 5x-3+2x^2 in standard form as 2x^2+5x-3 (degree 2 first). NY A-SSE.1 and A-REI.4 require standard form before using formulas like the quadratic formula. The coefficients a, b, c are read directly. Regents items present a quadratic in non-standard form and require rearranging before solving — always move everything to one side equal to zero.

questions.prompt: A polynomial of degree 2, written as ax^2+bx+c, that may be factored or simplified but does not necessarily equal zero.
questions.explanation: Quadratic expressions are degree-2 polynomials. Worked examples: x^2-7x+10, 3x^2+5, and 4x^2-9 are all quadratic expressions. NY A-SSE.1 covers interpretation. A quadratic expression becomes a quadratic equation when set equal to zero (or to another expression). Regents items distinguish expressions (to simplify/factor) from equations (to solve). Multiply, factor, or evaluate an expression; solve an equation.

questions.prompt: A point where the graph of a function crosses the x-axis, equivalent to a real root of the function.
questions.explanation: X-intercepts are the visible roots on a graph. Worked example: y=x^2-5x+6 has x-intercepts at (2, 0) and (3, 0), matching the factored form (x-2)(x-3). NY F-IF.7 and A-APR.3 cover this. Set y=0 and solve to find x-intercepts. Regents items often show a parabola with marked x-intercepts and ask students to construct the equation in factored form — multiply (x - x_intercept_1)(x - x_intercept_2) and check the vertex.

questions.prompt: A rectangular grid representation of polynomial multiplication where each row is a term of one polynomial and each column is a term of the other.
questions.explanation: Area models visualize distribution. Worked example: (x+3)(x+5) drawn as a 2-by-2 grid: cells contain x^2, 5x, 3x, 15; sum is x^2+8x+15. NY A-APR.1 supports this visual approach. The box method scales to larger polynomials, making it useful for trinomial-by-trinomial multiplication. Regents items rarely show the box explicitly, but it is a reliable strategy. The model directly inverts to factoring.

questions.prompt: Two numbers whose product equals c and whose sum equals b in factoring a trinomial x^2+bx+c into (x+p)(x+q).
questions.explanation: Factor pairs unlock trinomial factoring. Worked example: factor x^2+7x+12 — find two numbers multiplying to 12 and summing to 7: 3 and 4. So x^2+7x+12 = (x+3)(x+4). NY A-SSE.2 implies this method. List factor pairs of c, then check sums. Negative coefficients: for x^2-5x+6, find pair multiplying to 6 and summing to -5: -2 and -3, so (x-2)(x-3). Regents items test this fluency.

questions.prompt: A recurring numerical relationship among a, b, c in trinomials that suggests a factoring strategy or a special form.
questions.explanation: Coefficient patterns hint at factoring approaches. Worked example: if a=1 and c is positive, look for factor pairs summing to b. If a is greater than 1, try the AC method (multiply a*c, find factors of that summing to b). NY A-SSE.2 covers strategy choice. Regents items use varying coefficient patterns to test method selection — simple monic trinomials, harder a-greater-than-1 trinomials, and special forms (difference of squares, perfect square trinomial).

questions.prompt: The arrangement of + and - signs in a trinomial that predicts the signs of the factors when factored.
questions.explanation: Sign patterns predict factor signs. Worked examples: x^2+5x+6 has both factors positive (x+2)(x+3); x^2-5x+6 has both negative (x-2)(x-3); x^2+x-6 has opposite signs (x+3)(x-2); x^2-x-6 has opposite signs (x-3)(x+2). NY A-SSE.2 implies these rules. The constant's sign indicates same vs opposite, and the middle's sign indicates which dominates. Regents items test factoring across all four sign patterns.

questions.prompt: An expression produced by valid algebraic manipulation (factoring, distributing, combining like terms) that has the same value as the original for all inputs.
questions.explanation: Equivalent expressions agree on every input. Worked example: 2(x+3)+4x and 6x+6 are equivalent — substitute x=5 to get 16 and 16. NY A-SSE.2-3 covers equivalence. Regents Part I items ask which choice is equivalent to a given expression. The fastest check is substituting a non-zero, non-one value (like x=2 or x=5) and computing both — equivalent expressions always match. Algebraic verification (distributing, factoring) confirms permanently.

questions.prompt: The U-shaped curve produced by graphing a quadratic function y=ax^2+bx+c, opening upward when a>0 and downward when a<0.
questions.explanation: Parabolas are quadratic graphs. Worked example: y=x^2 has vertex (0,0) and opens upward; y=-x^2+4 has vertex (0,4) and opens downward. NY F-IF.7 requires graphing parabolas. Key features: vertex (highest or lowest point), axis of symmetry (vertical line through the vertex), x-intercepts (zero, one, or two), y-intercept (the c in standard form). Regents items always include at least one parabola graph or equation per exam.

questions.prompt: A strategy for solving quadratic equations by writing the polynomial as a product of binomials and applying the Zero Product Property.
questions.explanation: Factoring solves quadratic equations when integer roots exist. Worked example: solve x^2-5x+6=0 by factoring as (x-2)(x-3)=0; ZPP gives x=2 or x=3. NY A-REI.4 covers this. Factoring is the preferred method when the quadratic factors nicely over the integers. Regents items test this as a 2-point Part II problem — show factoring, ZPP application, and both solutions to earn full credit.

questions.prompt: The point where a parabola turns — the minimum if a>0 or maximum if a<0 — located at x = -b/(2a) for standard form.
questions.explanation: Vertex is the parabola's turning point. Worked example: y=x^2-6x+5 has vertex at x = -(-6)/(2*1) = 3, then y = 9-18+5 = -4, so vertex (3, -4). NY F-IF.4 and F-IF.7 require finding vertices. In vertex form y=a(x-h)^2+k, the vertex is (h, k) directly. Regents items ask for vertex coordinates and their meaning in context (maximum profit, peak height of a projectile).

questions.prompt: The vertical line x = -b/(2a) passing through the vertex, around which the parabola is mirror-symmetric.
questions.explanation: Axis of symmetry passes through the vertex. Worked example: for y=x^2-8x+12, axis is x = -(-8)/(2*1) = 4. NY F-IF.4 covers this feature. The axis equation is always x = constant. Points equidistant from the axis on the parabola have equal y-values — this symmetry helps locate additional points. Regents items frequently test this for sketching parabolas and identifying their key features.

questions.prompt: The smallest y-value on a parabola opening upward, equal to the y-coordinate of the vertex.
questions.explanation: Minimum occurs at the vertex when a>0. Worked example: y=x^2-4x+5 — vertex x = -(-4)/2 = 2, y = 4-8+5 = 1, so minimum is 1 at x=2. NY F-IF.4 covers extrema. In context, minimum might represent the lowest cost or smallest distance. Regents items provide quadratic models and ask 'What is the minimum value?' or 'When does the minimum occur?' Both questions reduce to finding the vertex coordinates.

questions.prompt: The closed-form solution x = (-b plus or minus sqrt(b^2-4ac)) / (2a) for solving any quadratic equation ax^2+bx+c=0.
questions.explanation: The quadratic formula always works. Worked example: solve x^2-6x+5=0 with a=1, b=-6, c=5 — x = (6 plus or minus sqrt(36-20))/2 = (6 plus or minus 4)/2, so x=5 or x=1. NY A-REI.4 explicitly cites this formula. It is provided on the NY Algebra 1 Reference Sheet during the Regents. Use it when factoring fails or roots are irrational. The discriminant under the radical tells how many real solutions exist.

questions.prompt: Another name for the vertex of a parabola — the precise location where the function transitions from increasing to decreasing or vice versa.
questions.explanation: Turning point synonymous with vertex. Worked example: y=-(x-2)^2+9 has its turning point at (2, 9), where the function changes from increasing to decreasing (max). NY F-IF.4 uses this terminology. Quadratics have exactly one turning point; cubics may have two; quartics may have three. Regents items use 'turning point' interchangeably with 'vertex' — calculate x=-b/(2a) and substitute back for y.

questions.prompt: The technique of rewriting ax^2+bx+c by adding and subtracting (b/2)^2 to produce a perfect-square trinomial inside a binomial-squared form.
questions.explanation: Completing the square converts to vertex form. Worked example: x^2-6x+5 — take half of -6 (which is -3), square (9), add and subtract: (x^2-6x+9)-9+5 = (x-3)^2-4. NY A-REI.4 covers this method. The resulting form (x-3)^2-4 reveals vertex (3,-4) directly. Regents items test completing the square for solving equations and converting to vertex form for graphing.

questions.prompt: Solving quadratic equations of the form (x-h)^2 = k by taking the square root of both sides, yielding x-h = plus or minus sqrt(k).
questions.explanation: Square root method works on isolated squared terms. Worked example: (x-3)^2 = 16 — take square root: x-3 = plus or minus 4, so x = 3 plus or minus 4 = 7 or -1. NY A-REI.4 includes this method. Don't forget the plus or minus — both roots must be reported. Regents items often present equations already in the form (x-h)^2=k or convert into this form via completing the square.

questions.prompt: The expression b^2-4ac inside the quadratic-formula radical that determines the number and type of solutions to ax^2+bx+c=0.
questions.explanation: Discriminant reveals solution count. Worked example: for x^2-4x+3=0, discriminant = 16-12 = 4 > 0, so 2 real solutions. For x^2-4x+4=0, discriminant = 0, so 1 real solution (a double root). For x^2+x+1=0, discriminant = 1-4 = -3 < 0, so no real solutions. NY A-REI.4 expects students to use this. Regents items test 'how many real solutions' — compute discriminant only, no need to find the solutions themselves.

questions.prompt: The quadratic form y=ax^2+bx+c, where a is the leading coefficient, b is the linear coefficient, and c is the y-intercept.
questions.explanation: Standard form makes y-intercept obvious. Worked example: y=2x^2-3x+5 has a=2, b=-3, c=5; the y-intercept is (0, 5). NY F-IF.7 requires graphing from standard form. Compute the axis of symmetry as x=-b/(2a), find vertex, then plot symmetric points. Regents items present quadratics in standard form for the quadratic formula and for identifying the y-intercept directly.

questions.prompt: The quadratic form y=a(x-h)^2+k, where (h, k) is the vertex of the parabola, useful for direct identification of the turning point.
questions.explanation: Vertex form puts the vertex on display. Worked example: y=3(x-2)^2-7 has vertex (2, -7) — read directly. NY F-IF.7 covers vertex form. Convert from standard via completing the square. The a value still indicates opening direction (a>0 up, a<0 down) and width (|a|>1 narrower, |a|<1 wider). Regents items provide vertex form to test direct extraction of (h, k) and to verify transformations from the parent y=x^2.

questions.prompt: The quadratic form y=a(x-p)(x-q), where p and q are the x-intercepts (zeros) of the parabola.
questions.explanation: Intercept form exposes the zeros. Worked example: y=2(x-1)(x+3) has x-intercepts (1, 0) and (-3, 0). NY F-IF.7 supports this form. The axis of symmetry is the average of p and q: x=(p+q)/2. Then substitute to find the vertex y. Regents items provide graphs with marked intercepts and ask students to write the equation in factored form — multiply (x - p)(x - q) and adjust a using a third point.

questions.prompt: The plus or minus sign on a in y=ax^2+bx+c, which determines whether the parabola opens upward (positive a) or downward (negative a).
questions.explanation: Leading coefficient sign predicts shape. Worked example: y=x^2 (a=1, positive) opens upward; y=-2x^2+5 (a=-2, negative) opens downward. NY F-IF.7 connects this to graph behavior. The vertex is a minimum when a>0 and a maximum when a<0. Regents items use leading coefficient sign to interpret context — a negative coefficient in a projectile motion model h=-16t^2+v0t+h0 reflects gravity pulling down.

questions.prompt: The c in standard-form y=ax^2+bx+c, equal to the y-intercept and to the value of y when x=0.
